## Summary
Can cosmological observations determine the absolute neutrino mass scale and hierarchy? Current upper bounds from CMB + BAO constrain Σmν < 0.12 eV (95% CL), approaching the minimum mass guaranteed by oscillation experiments (~0.06 eV for normal hierarchy). A detection would bridge particle physics and cosmology.

## Evidence
Planck CMB data combined with DESI BAO yield Σmν < 0.072 eV (95% CL), which is in tension with the minimum mass from oscillation experiments under the inverted hierarchy (Σmν ≥ 0.10 eV). This tentative result, if confirmed, would establish the normal hierarchy cosmologically. However, the bound depends on the assumed dark energy model — allowing w0-wa freedom weakens it significantly.

## Significance
The interplay between neutrino mass bounds and dark energy constraints is a critical systematic in modern cosmology. DESI + Euclid combined analyses may achieve a 3σ detection of the minimum neutrino mass, but only if dark energy model assumptions are well controlled. The question of whether cosmology can robustly measure neutrino mass remains open.
